chapters.title: Epidemics do not cross every border the same way
chapters.text:
Social epidemics happen when ideas or behaviors spread like wildfire. They look unruly and unpredictable, do not they? Yet this belief does not hold up to scrutiny after a closer look at the data.

Take Illinois and Indiana as an example. If we compare their incomes and unemployment levels, the numbers are roughly the same. Yet these neighboring states are drastically different in terms of prescribed opioid painkillers. In 2006, when the opioid epidemic started, Illinois residents took 366 doses per person in a year, while in Indiana the number was 756.6. Whatever drove the opioid epidemic eased at the state line. Why?

Social epidemics are not chaotic. They rest on certain principles. In 2000, Malcolm Gladwell described some of these principles in his first book, The Tipping Point, and analyzed how we can use them to drive positive changes. Almost twenty-five years later, he returns to the topic with less optimism to address the revenge of the tipping point, its dark side, and its use for social engineering.

The more interconnected the world becomes, the more influential our actions are.

What sets Illinois and Indiana apart? Which three elements make up social epidemics? How can each of us contribute to them? It is time to face the unsettling truth.

chapters.title: Big stories shape what we all believe
chapters.text:
Stories matter, but overstories, the first element of social epidemics, matter even more. They are the up-in-the-air beliefs that shape our attitudes whether we realize it or not. LGBT people know the power of overstories from their own experience.

In the early 1980s, attorney Evan Wolfson tried to figure out how to reduce discrimination against gay people, which he believed existed because people could not accept whom they love. So he proposed changing the perception of gay love to ease biases. Yet how to do it? His answer was to legalize gay marriage.

Society's overstory was far from ready for this move at the time. Most Americans learned about LGBT people from books and TV, which failed to give accurate representation. According to Professor Bonnie Dow's research, most 1980s TV narratives about gay people rested on three principles: gay characters did not get much screen time, their gender identity was treated as a problem other characters had to solve, and TV programs never showed communities of gay people.

Television was a far more powerful tool for shaping attitudes then than it is now. For instance, let us compare how many Americans watched the final episodes of two popular series, The Big Bang Theory in 2019 and M*A*S*H in 1983. Eighteen million watched the Big Bang finale, compared to M*A*S*H's 106 million viewers, almost half of the American audience. That is striking, right?

Even when you feel like you are losing, keep going. You never know when your efforts approach a game-changing point.

Supporters of legalizing gay marriage ran campaigns year after year but had little support among Americans. Activists were ready to give up. Yet in 2012, gay marriage suddenly started to win on the ballot. When activists interviewed people who backed their campaign, most mentioned the impact of television. What were they referring to?

Will & Grace, which started in 1998, tells how two gay men, lawyer Will and his friend Jack, share an apartment with Grace and her assistant Karen. Although the series was far from an exact representation of gay people, it transformed the narrative about them. Gays were at the center of the story and were not alone. No one in the series treated their gender identity as a problem. Gays succeeded in society.

Many factors prepared this shift in overstory, but Will & Grace was a game-changer.

The 1978 Holocaust miniseries was a tipping point for most Americans to learn about that Nazi atrocity.

chapters.title: Diversity makes any group more resilient
chapters.text:
We love monocultures, do not we? When everyone around us shares our values, goals, and beliefs, we feel more comfortable and confident. The problem is that epidemics also love monoculture. Why? A lack of diversity makes a community more vulnerable.

Monocultures are rare. The default state for most natural systems is diversity.

Many of us have heard about towns where affluent working-class Americans live. Sociologists Seth Abrutyn and Anna Mueller have studied one of them, which they call Poplar Grove. In this close-knit community, everyone values success and social popularity.

It is no surprise that the local school is among the most prestigious in the country. However, some residents prefer to drive extra miles so their children can study in nearby towns instead. Why? From 2007 to 2015, nineteen Poplar Grove High School teenagers committed suicide. What caused these tragic events? Gladwell argues that monoculture has created a high-pressure environment.

There were rebels, teenagers who did not want to study at Ivy League schools or be famous athletes. But their environment made them doubt their values. They saw no other behavior models and started wondering if something was wrong with them. In a success-oriented mindset, insecurity looks like weakness, and teenagers become harder on themselves.

Beyond the dangers of high pressure, this story teaches another essential lesson. Diversity does not just mean including people who think differently. Their numbers matter too, which leads us to the second element of epidemics, group proportions. This principle applies across fields, including business.

In the 1970s, one company hired female sales specialists, expecting to benefit from the so-called woman effect. Female workers are well known for collaborating more eagerly, asking more questions, and being excellent listeners. Yet when these women failed in the sales department, prominent sociologist Rosabeth Moss Kanter decided to investigate why.

You are not the only one who wants to have a voice in opposition to what the majority believes. Speak up.

It turned out that these saleswomen were highly talented. The problem was skewed group proportions. These sales teams included one woman and ten male specialists. The women were treated as representatives of a specific gender, not as unique personalities. When a female specialist succeeded, her colleagues chalked it up to her attractiveness, not her talent.

This finding raised a question: how many people with different beliefs should a community have to benefit from diversity?

chapters.title: The Magic Third can flip any group
chapters.text:
Epidemics do not unfold steadily. They start slowly, but everything transforms overnight after a specific event, a tipping point. Sometimes, we can predict these moments. Let us take Professor Damon Centola's experiment as an example.

Centola created an online name game in which he divided participants into pairs. He showed them a photo of a stranger and asked them to type in the stranger's possible name. Afterward, the pair saw what each other wrote and played again with another team, using the same photo. The game ended when the answers coincided. It sounds like an infinite process, right? Yet the game usually lasted around fifteen exchanges.

People excel at figuring out and adjusting to social norms.

Then Centola introduced a twist. For instance, when participants agreed on Jessica, a particular group of rebels intentionally typed in another name, such as Lily. Centola's research question was, how many rebels can shift the majority in a new direction? The input of five, ten, or twenty percent of rebels did not influence the group. Yet everyone started to write Lily as soon as rebels made up twenty-five percent. That is how the Magic Quarter works.

Sometimes, this number should be thirty-three percent, the Magic Third, as studies of the women effect in corporate boards prove. In sum, twenty-five to thirty-three percent is often the watershed for significant, sudden transformation. This knowledge seems crucial for promoting positive changes and preventing negative ones. However, the case of Palo Alto proves that is easier said than done.

In the 1950s, many Black people left the South, trying to avoid Jim Crow laws. Yet as soon as they settled in a new place, white people moved out en masse, a phenomenon called white flight. The tipping point for leaving was when African Americans made up twenty-five to thirty-three percent of the community.

Citizens of Palo Alto wanted to create an alternative. They built twenty-four residential lots and sold them to eight white, Black, and Asian families under one condition: they would leave the house to people of the same race when they moved out. The experiment was an enormous success. People of different races had picnics together and helped each other.

The community lived in harmony until one white family decided to sell their house to African Americans to support them during a crisis. As a result, residents of the other twenty-three houses faced a dilemma. Should they allow the sale, which would shift the balance, or prohibit it? After a long and fierce discussion, they put balance first despite feeling remorse and forbade the sale.

chapters.title: Harvard's dark history with quotas
chapters.text:
Palo Alto's citizens were clear about their intentions and cooperated with residents to create a thriving community. However, in some societies, people manipulate group proportions in secret.

At the beginning of the twentieth century, more than a third of Columbia University's undergraduates were Jewish. Many Ivy League colleges saw this percentage as a threat and started searching for ways to control group proportions. For instance, Harvard University made its exams more difficult. Yet Jewish students passed these harder tests with flying colors.

So the university's president, Abbott Lawrence Lowell, introduced a new admission system based on subjective criteria. Suddenly, students' after-school activities started to matter, and personal interviews became obligatory. All these measures had one aim, to keep the number of Jewish students at Harvard at around fifteen percent.

The perception of Jewish people has changed dramatically since then. However, some warning tendencies remain. Consider statistics about Caltech, a prestigious university that accepts students only because of their academic records. The number of Asian students there has fluctuated from twenty-five to forty-two percent over the last few decades. At Harvard, that number has stayed under twenty percent.

If we are to protect the integrity of our institutions, we need to be made aware of the games being played below the surface.

A two-track admission system is another warning sign. The first track is for most students. The second one is for children of Harvard professors or alums, wealthy people, and athletes.

What is the difference? Data from Students for Fair Admissions speaks for itself. In 2014, applicants from the first track with slightly lower study results had a ten percent chance of enrolling in Harvard. Yet those with the same grades who used the second track had a fifty percent chance of acceptance.

Gladwell believes this two-track system is another tool for controlling group proportions. Professors will be satisfied, and alums and wealthy people will donate money to Harvard if their children study there. Yet what about athletes?

Math provides the answer. For instance, a tennis trainer costs five thousand to eighteen thousand dollars a year. The conclusion becomes even more obvious once you add the costs of memberships, equipment, and physical therapy. Collegiate athletes aiming to play professionally should have a lot of money, so accepting them will make the university appear more elite.

Colleges should earn their prestige through academic achievements, not through the number of wealthy students they enroll.

chapters.title: A few people can move the whole crowd
chapters.text:
As we have seen with the screenwriters of Will & Grace and Harvard's former president, individual decisions can promote or prevent change. Yet the human factor becomes even more visible with the third element of social epidemics, superspreaders. The opioid crisis highlights their role vividly.

In 1939, California Bureau of Narcotic Enforcement Director Paul E. Madden realized that the battle against drugs should go beyond illegal foreign substances. Opioid painkillers can also be misused when unscrupulous doctors overprescribe them or when criminals forge blank prescriptions. So Madden advised authorities to print blanks in triplicate, for doctors, patients, and the Bureau of Narcotic Enforcement.

This control encouraged doctors to choose medications more thoughtfully, which changed the overstory of opioid painkillers. The results were so effective that many states introduced the same change.

Half a century later, Dr. Russell Portenoy offered another overstory. He claimed that opioids save people from pain, so doctors should prescribe them more often. His ideas became so widespread that most US states supported him. The exceptions were the triplicate states, Idaho, New York, Texas, California, and Illinois. Remember our example of Indiana and Illinois in the first chapter? The difference in overstory about drug prescriptions is what caused the epidemic to ease at the border.

To predict the future of a community, look at its overstories.

Purdue Pharma took full advantage of this trend in the 1990s and launched OxyContin, a painkiller that is easy to abuse. The company heavily marketed it to non-triplicate states. Those states faced a higher rate of overdose deaths and violent crimes, a decrease in economic growth, and a decline in infant health. Yet it was just the beginning.

The opioid crisis entered a new phase at the beginning of the 2000s when McKinsey & Company's journal featured a publication on pharmaceutical sales. Its author called current methods outdated and offered a new approach. Purdue got interested, and together, they identified doctors who prescribed OxyContin far more often than others (sometimes ten times more) and focused their marketing of oxycodone on them. This method worked. Sometimes, doctors' prescriptions rocketed from three hundred to twelve hundred a year. These doctors are what Gladwell calls superspreaders of the epidemic.

The culmination of the opioid crisis came when Purdue made the drug less easy to abuse. It was a too-late measure. Its patients just shifted to more dangerous illegal drugs.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
How would the attitude toward LGBT people have changed had an unbiased series appeared earlier? Would the college admission process be the same if Lowell had not manipulated group proportions? What if Purdue Pharma had never found out about superspreaders? Addressing social epidemics in terms of someone's responsibility raises many unsettling questions. Yet it is essential, if not the only, way to minimize negative transformations and promote positive social epidemics.

A few habits to try. Determine the overstories about success in your community. Do they reflect your actual values? Speak to more people with different backgrounds to benefit from diversity. Explore Headway's summary of Contagious: Why Things Catch On to understand why some ideas stick.
